title: Remove vs abandon oil tank
summary: Cross-state guide for cases where the tank is confirmed and the next choice is disposition.
quickAnswer: Removal versus abandonment is state-specific and should come after confirmation, not before.
stateEntryPrompt: Choose a state route for disposition guidance.
introPoints: Confirm the tank and the state process before comparing closure options.
Use records and leak indicators to avoid collapsing different routes into one.
Use this guide to compare the route only after the tank is confirmed and the state closure path is in view.
whatNotToAssume: Do not assume removal is always required.
Do not assume abandonment in place is always allowed.
Do not assume this is still only a transaction question once a leak is suspected.
bestFor: Tank presence is confirmed and the next decision is how the state allows closure to happen.
The route already moved past simple suspicion and now needs a state-specific disposition answer.
A contractor or record stack shows the question is no longer just buyer-seller timing.
beforeYouCall: Confirm the tank and the state closure path before comparing disposition options.
Pull any closure, assessment, or site-condition document before asking whether removal is mandatory.
Keep leak concern separate from ordinary closure planning.
documentsThatMatter: State or delegated-local closure guidance, contractor assessments, and tank-condition notes.
Any prior permit, closure record, or invoice showing earlier work on the tank.
Any leak, contamination, or cleanup document that limits the available options.
whenToEscalate: Move to leak-and-cleanup when contamination concern appears or cleanup paperwork already exists.
Move back to records-first if the tank is still not actually confirmed.
Stay state-specific because one state's allowed closure path may not match another's.

sourceStack.title: Assessing Contamination at Residential Underground Heating Oil Tank Closures
